qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: The core Hebrew terms a.sham (H0816) and a.von (H5771G) directly name inner states arising from wrongdoing: a.sham encodes the moment of realising guilt (Lev 5:2-4; Gen 42:21), and a.von names the iniquity that weighs on the person (Psa 38:4; 51:2, 5). The Greek suneidēsis (conscience, G4893) makes the inner moral-monitoring dimension explicit across the NT, functioning as the NT's primary term for the internal faculty that produces guilt-awareness.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Qal: to do wrong, offend, trespass, be or become guilty, be held guilty, be incriminated; Niphal: to suffer punishment; Hiphil: to declare guilty. TWOT extension: to be desolate, acknowledge offense. The verb that names the act of entering the guilt-state and the recognition of that state.

Legal charge, accusation, crime; reason, cause, motive. LSJ primary: responsibility, guilt, blame, or their imputation. Used in the trial narratives of Jesus for Pilate's verdict of no guilt. The legal register of guilt as objective charge.

Culpable, responsible; the accused, the culprit (as noun); the one responsible for something. LSJ primary: culpable, bearing responsibility. Used in the trial narrative of Jesus for Pilate's verdict of no guilt.

Consciousness; a persisting moral impression; conscience as the inward faculty judging one's actions and principles (Rom 2:15; 9:1); conscience as the inward moral and spiritual frame (Tit 1:15; Heb 9:14; 10:22). Can be good/clear, weak, defiled, evil, or seared. The primary NT inner-being term for the moral monitoring faculty.

Freely, for nothing; without cause, undeservedly; in vain, for no purpose. Relevant to guilt as the counter-concept: used for unjust accusation (Psa 35:7; 69:4; 109:3), the guiltlessness of the persecuted (Psa 119:161; Job 2:3), and the shedding of innocent blood without cause (1Sa 25:31; 1Ki 2:31).

Guilt, offense, guiltiness; compensation for offense; the guilt/trespass offering. Covers both the inner state (guilt) and the cultic remedy that addresses it. Isa 53:10 is the most significant CORE verse: when his soul makes an offering for guilt (a.sham) — the Servant's soul as the guilt offering.

Guiltiness, guilt, offense, wrong-doing; doing wrong, committing a trespass; the state of being guilty. Most significant cluster: Ezr 9:6-15 — Ezra's corporate confession where ash.mah is the named state of accumulated national guilt.

Guilty, faulty — and obliged to offer a guilt-offering. The adjectival form naming the state of being in the condition of guilt. Gen 42:21 (the Joseph brothers) is the most significant inner-being instance.

Perversity, depravity, iniquity; guilt of iniquity; consequence or punishment of iniquity. Derived from root a.vah (to twist, to pervert). The single term encompasses the act of wrongdoing, the resulting guilt-state, and the punishment/consequence — making it the most semantically dense term in the set. Most significant CORE cluster: the Psalms of personal guilt-confession (Psa 25:11; 32:2, 5; 38:4, 18; 40:12; 51:2, 5, 9; 130:3); the Servant Song (Isa 53:5, 6, 11); Ezekiel's individual responsibility texts (Eze 18); Ezra's confession (Ezr 9:6-7).

Sin, wrongdoing; guilt or imputation of sin (Joh 9:41); a principle or cause of sin, sinful propensity (Rom 7:17, 20); a guilty subject or sin-offering (2Cor 5:21). Covers the act, the inner principle, the guilt, and the remedy. Paul's description of hamartia as an indwelling power (Rom 7:17-23) is distinctive and has no parallel in OT sin vocabulary.
Sin; sin offering. Used often with the intensifier 'great sin' (Exo 32:21, 31). Smaller occurrence noun form of the cha.ta root.
Sin; guilt for sin; punishment for sin. Noun form of the missing-the-mark root. Key CORE verse: Isa 53:12 (bore the sin of many); Isa 1:18 (though your sins are like scarlet); Psa 51:4; Lam 3:39.

To lift, bear up, carry; to bear/carry continuously; to take away, carry off, forgive; Hiphil: to cause to bear iniquity. The guilt-relevant sub-entry covers the idiom of bearing iniquity as a weight (Lev 5:1; Isa 53:12) and God's lifting it away (Psa 32:5; Mic 7:18). The same root covers both accumulation and removal of guilt.

Qal: to sin, miss the way, offend; Piel: to purify from sin; Hiphil: to cause to sin, lead into sin, make to sin; also to bear a loss. The primary OT verb for sinning used for individual act (David, Pharaoh, Achan), national pattern (Jeroboam's sin), and the confession sequence I have sinned.

No STEP block available — listed in primary term header only. Gloss: guilty, liable, subject to judgment. Etymology: from enechō (to be held in), suggesting being held fast under obligation or legal liability. Source file notes Mat 5:21-22 and Heb 2:15 as primary references.

No STEP block available — listed in primary term header only. Gloss: condemnation, the verdict of guilt. Noun from katakrinō (to judge against, condemn). Source file identifies Rom 8:1 (no katakrima in Christ Jesus) as the theological resolution of guilt — a key absence given its doctrinal centrality.

verse_classification.reason: Legal/consequential contexts; inner guilt dimension present but indirect
Prophetic judgment contexts; inner dimension present but national
No named inner guilt state — character description, economic context, or animal suffering
Legal charge inscription — injustice context; inner guilt dimension indirect
Legal procedures, reasons for actions, causal connectors — no guilt referent
No cause for the riot — civic context; inner dimension indirect through the threat of charges
Source of eternal salvation — positive causal/theological; no guilt referent
Gratis, in vain, without cause in commercial/legal/prophetic contexts — no guilt referent
Guilt offering as remedy; inner dimension present through the concept of guilt requiring cultic address
Ritual prescriptions for guilt offering — cultic/procedural; inner guilt presupposed but not named
National guilt incurred by apostasy — collective; inner dimension present but national
Legal provision for realized guilt; Guilt of Samaria as idol name — ritual/legal/ironic; no direct inner guilt focus
Generational transmission and national indictment — collective/theological; inner dimension present but at national/doctrinal scale
National iniquity and consequences in Jeremiah/Lamentations — collective; inner dimension present but corporate
Prophetic accountability and national iniquity — inner dimension present but at national/figurative scale
Mixed lament/praise contexts — divine pardon or corporate confession; inner dimension varies
Corporate confession and prophetic indictment — collective; inner dimension indirect
Ritual bearing of iniquity — cultic regulation; inner guilt presupposed but procedural focus
Iniquity as stumbling block, on the bones, as ruin — figurative/prophetic summary; inner dimension indirect or absent
Forgiveness of sins in gospel ministry — soteriological; inner guilt present but focus is divine action
Proclamation of forgiveness in Acts — evangelistic; corporate/soteriological focus
Theological summaries of redemption from sin — inner dimension present but primarily doctrinal or Christological
Priestly sacrifice for sins, eschatological — cultic/ritual/theological; inner guilt presupposed but not the focus
Great sin brought on a group — collective; inner dimension present but corporate
Sin offering not required — cultic/theological; no inner guilt focus
Legal provisions for bearing sin, juridical/cultic — inner guilt presupposed but not the focus
Ritual bearing of iniquity/sin — cultic regulation; inner guilt presupposed but procedural focus
Bearing consequences, bearing blame, symbolic bearing — figurative/legal or prophetic-symbolic; inner dimension varies
Corporate/national confession — collective; inner dimension present but communal
National/prophetic sin and lament — collective; inner dimension present but corporate
Collective national sin and instruction — communal; inner dimension present but corporate
Jeroboam and kings' narrative pattern — historical; no inner guilt focus
Ritual sin offerings and purification — cultic/legal; inner guilt presupposed but procedural
The sinner as a wisdom/social category — no inner guilt state focus

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: ash.mah / a.von in Ezr 9:6-7 and Dan 9:8
cross_registry_links.note: The confession texts in Ezra 9 and Daniel 9 pair guilt directly with shame — 'I am ashamed and blush' (Ezr 9:6) and 'open shame belongs to us' (Dan 9:8). Guilt and shame are co-experienced in these texts.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.sham in Hos 5:15; a.von in Lev 26:41
cross_registry_links.note: Acknowledged guilt functions as the trigger for seeking God (Hos 5:15: until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face) and for humbling (Lev 26:41: uncircumcised heart humbled and they make amends for iniquity). Guilt connects to Humility as its proper inner response.

Psa 31:10 (life spent with sorrow, years with sighing, strength fails because of iniquity) and Psa 38:4, 18 (iniquity over my head like a heavy burden; I confess my iniquity, I am sorry for my sin) show guilt-iniquity producing grief and physical deterioration.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.von / cha.ta in lament Psalms; Eze 7:16
cross_registry_links.note: The lament Psalms regularly pair distress with iniquity confession. Psa 38 is the clearest example — distress from guilt producing somatic symptoms. Eze 7:16 uses a.von in the context of those who escape the sword mourning, each over his iniquity.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: hamartia as enslaving power in Rom 6
cross_registry_links.note: Paul's description of sin as a dominating power — sin reigned, slaves to sin, sin dwells within — overlaps with the orientation of the inner person away from God that characterises Pride in the word set.

cross_registry_links.connecting_term: a.von connected to divine wrath (Exo 20:5; Num 14:18; Ezr 9:13-15; Dan 9:16)
cross_registry_links.note: The a.von verse set consistently links iniquity to divine anger/wrath as the consequence — the iniquity → wrath pattern runs through Exodus, Numbers, Ezra, and Daniel's confession.

data_quality_flags.description: enochos (guilty, liable) appears in the primary term header (line 12) and in the source file's STEP suggestion as the primary NT legal guilt term but has no STEP block in the body. No verse data or meaning block is available in the source file.

katakrima (condemnation, verdict of guilt) appears in the primary term header (line 13) and is identified in the source file's own STEP suggestion as the theological resolution of guilt (Rom 8:1) but has no STEP block in the body. This is a significant gap given Rom 8:1 is the most important NT guilt-resolution verse.

a.sham appears as two entries: H0816 (verb: be guilty, 35x) and H0817 (noun: guilt offering, 46x). These share a root and related words list but are functionally distinct: H0816 names the act of entering the guilt-state; H0817 names both the guilt-state and the cultic remedy. Kept separate: verse contexts confirm genuine differentiation.

a.shem (guilty adjective) has only 3 occurrences. The source file provides 2 verse references (Gen 42:21; 2Sa 14:13). Both are CORE and exegetically significant, but the small verse set limits pattern analysis.

a.von covers three distinct semantic layers simultaneously: (1) the act of wrongdoing (iniquity as perversity from the root a.vah to twist), (2) the resulting guilt-state, and (3) the consequence/punishment. Many verses deploy all three layers. This density distinguishes a.von from narrower terms like chet (primarily act/guilt) or ash.mah (primarily the guilt-state).

data_quality_flags.affected_term: na.sa (H5375J) — lifting as both accumulation and removal
data_quality_flags.description: na.sa uses the same lifting metaphor for both bearing guilt (accumulation: Lev 5:1; Isa 53:12) and God's lifting/forgiving guilt (removal: Psa 32:5; Mic 7:18; Zec 3:4). The same root covers both the weight of guilt and its removal. This structural feature of Hebrew should be preserved for Phase 2.

The cha.ta / cha.ta.ah blocks appear to have been reproduced twice in the source file (visible in the verse list starting at line 1700 and earlier). Classification has been performed on the unique verse set only. The duplication does not affect the analysis.
